questions.text: A joey is
When a joey is born
Being in a pouch keeps a joey
Joeys are compared to jelly beans because of their
At about ten months old a joey
options: does not live in the pouch anymore.
leaves its mother to live in a new place.
lives in the pouch when it needs to feel safe.
has become a fully grown kangaroo.

questions.text: What is this text mainly about?
options: how much joeys eat and drink
what joeys’ early lives are like

questions.text: Who made the mess?
Where is Planet Mess?
Bor-ing, he thought. What does Nick think is Bor-ing?
What does Nick use as a space suit?
Nick joins the vacuum cleaner to
Where does Nick put the toys from his toy bucket?
Eva Leung is a cartoonist. Where does Eva regularly publish her work?
There are two cartooning classes on the program because
options: more time is needed to learn cartooning.
the classes are for different age groups.
there is a strict limit on numbers for each class.
some children might want to do both the classes.

questions.text: Harry Pope is a writer. What other word is used in the text to describe his job?
The activity with Harry Pope is free. This is most likely because
options: there are no materials needed for that activity.
the library does not charge for activities on Wednesdays.
Harry Pope has not published any books yet.
the Harry Pope activity is shorter than the others.

questions.text: This poster is aimed at
On which day are there two different activities?
Which of these rules at Bridge Park is not shown in a picture at the bottom of the sign?
options: No loud music or noise is allowed.

questions.text: The people who designed the Bridge Park sign probably think that
options: parks are a good way to raise money.
use of the park needs to be controlled.

questions.text: What is the one thing both signs ask people to do?
The words stare at the sky, smell our flowers and hug the trees suggest
options: that this is the order in which things should be done at the park.
that people can use many senses to experience the park.
that group activities are encouraged at the park.
that there is not much to do in the park.

questions.text: Help us keep it this way by putting all rubbish in the bin before you leave. The use of us and you in this quotation helps to show
options: that the park belongs to the reader.
that responsibility for the park is shared.
that everyone agrees with the writer.
that the park is visited by many people.

questions.text: The Bellevue Park sign helps park users by
Which of these best describes the difference between the two parks?
What is the problem in the text that needs to be solved?
options: The wise man can’t think of a good idea.
The brave chief keeps getting sore feet.
The servants are tired from working too hard.
The maiden disappears and can’t be found.

questions.text: What did the brave chief wear on his feet before the wise man’s invention?
What is wrong with the wise man’s first two solutions?
options: They limit where the chief can go.
They make the chief look foolish.

questions.text: Which of the wise man’s suggestions made the chief feel embarrassed?
What type of text is this?
What main question does the text answer?
options: When were the first moccasins made?
How were the first moccasins made?
Why were the first moccasins made?
Where were the first moccasins made?

questions.text: In the first paragraph, Jack is
options: holding onto the side of the boat.
lying on the bottom of the boat.
huddled at the back of the boat.
sitting at the front of the boat.

questions.text: What effect did the cold have on Jack?
options: It made it hard for him to breathe.

questions.text: According to the text, what particularly frightens Jack?
options: being unable to see the shore
being knocked out of the boat
being unable to find his oars
being blown off course by wild winds

questions.text:
... towering mountains ... (paragraph 1)
The writer uses this phrase to emphasise the

It was more like a deep rumble. (paragraph 3)
In this quotation, It refers to the sound of the
Which of these describes the purposes of paragraphs 1 and 3 in this text?
options: presents a turning point | resolves a complication
presents a complication | describes new characters
presents a turning point | introduces a new complication
presents a complication | sets up a turning point

questions.text: Which word has the same meaning as eerie in the text? (paragraph 3)
... he might even live to tell this strange tale. (paragraph 3) What does this quotation suggest about Jack’s thinking?
options: Jack is now sure his story will end well.
Jack thinks his situation may be improving.
Jack has started composing his story in his mind.
Jack knows no-one will believe what he has been through.

questions.text: What did Sara plan to do on Saturday morning?
